item.title: Designing Go APIs the Standard Library Way: Accept Interfaces, Return Structs
item.author: Shrijith Venkatramana
item.content:encoded:
Go’s standard library is a masterclass in clean, pragmatic API design. One of its core principles is accept interfaces, return structs. This approach ensures flexibility for inputs, rich functionality for outputs, and code that’s easy to test and extend. In this article, we’ll dive into what this principle means, why it works, and how you can apply it to write better Go APIs. I will share practical examples, standard library insights, and tips to avoid common pitfalls—so that you can put these insights to use immediately in your projects.
Power Outlets and Devices: A Practical Analogy for Go’s Design To grasp why accept interfaces, return structs is so effective, think of a and an :Interface as a Power Outlet: A power outlet defines a standard (e.g., voltage and plug shape). Any device with a compatible plug can connect, regardless of whether it’s a lamp, a laptop charger, or a blender. In Go, an interface like io.Reader works the same way—it accepts any type that implements its methods, giving functions flexibility to work with diverse inputs.: Once plugged in, the device (e.g., a blender) offers its full range of features—blend, pulse, or chop. Similarly, a struct returned by a function provides complete access to its fields and methods, letting users leverage its full capabilities.
What Does "Accept Interfaces, Return Structs" Mean in Practice? In Go, APIs often as function parameters and as results. This design choice balances flexibility with functionality:: Allow any type that implements the interface’s methods, making the function versatile.: Provide full access to the returned type’s fields and methods, giving users maximum control.
Example: A Simple Processor Here’s a basic example to illustrate:: interface, allowing any type with a method (e.g., , , or even a file).: struct, exposing its field and any methods you might add later.This pattern lets work with diverse inputs while returning a struct with clear, usable functionality.
Why This Pattern Works: Interfaces Hide, Structs Expose Interfaces and structs serve complementary roles in Go’s type system. Understanding their strengths helps explain why this principle is effective.
Interfaces: Abstraction and Flexibility An interface defines a contract—a set of methods—without revealing how they’re implemented. This makes them ideal for function inputs because:They allow to satisfy the same requirement.They make easy for testing.They keep APIs from specific implementations.For example, is satisfied by files, network connections, or in-memory buffers, all without changing the function’s signature.
Structs: Transparency and Power Structs, on the other hand, are concrete types that expose their fields and methods. Returning a struct gives users: to the type’s capabilities., as new methods can be added without breaking existing code., since the type’s implementation is explicit.
Comparison Table: Interfaces vs. Structs Provides concrete implementationHigh (any type implementing methods)Limited to defined methodsNew methods/fields can be addedMay need an interface for mockingExposes fields and methods: Use interfaces for inputs to keep functions flexible and testable. Return structs to give users full control over the output.
Real-World Examples from Go’s Standard Library The Go standard library consistently applies this principle. Let’s break down some examples to see it in action.
1. : Flexible Body, Rich Request : for the request body, allowing streams like , , or even .: , a struct with fields like , , and , plus methods like .This design lets you pass any readable stream as the body while giving you a fully-featured to manipulate.
2. : Stream JSON with Ease : , supporting any source of JSON data (e.g., files, HTTP responses).: , a struct with methods like and for fine-grained JSON processing.
3. : Buffered Reading Made Simple : , such as a file or network connection.: , a struct with methods like and for efficient buffered reading.
4. : Sorting Any Collection : , requiring , , and methods, allowing custom types to be sorted.: None (sorts in-place), but the interface ensures flexibility.These examples show how the standard library uses interfaces for broad compatibility and structs for rich functionality.: Check out Effective Go for deeper insights into Go’s design philosophy.
Best Practices for Applying This Principle To design APIs that feel “Go-like,” follow these guidelines:: Use small interfaces (e.g., , ) to maximize compatibility. A single-method interface is often enough.Return Structs for Extensibility: Structs allow you to add methods or fields later without breaking existing code.: Interfaces as inputs make it easy to mock dependencies in tests.Avoid Premature Interfaces: Don’t define interfaces until you have multiple implementations or a clear need for abstraction.: Clearly document what the returned struct’s methods do and how to use them.
Example: Building a Logging API Let’s design a logging API following this principle:: , allowing logs to go to files, stdout, or network streams.: , a struct with a method (and room to add more methods later).This API is flexible, testable, and extensible, aligning with Go’s philosophy.
Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them While this principle is powerful, it’s easy to misapply. Here are mistakes to watch out for:: Returning an interface (e.g., ) limits users to its methods, hiding additional functionality.: Return a struct instead, letting users access all fields and methods.: Defining interfaces when only one implementation exists adds complexity.: Let consumers define interfaces if needed, or wait until you have multiple implementations.: Accepting structs as input makes mocking harder.: Use interfaces to allow mock implementations in tests.: If you’re unsure, study the standard library—it’s a goldmine of practical examples.
Why This Matters for Your Go Projects Adopting accept interfaces, return structs makes your Go APIs:: They work with a wide range of inputs.: Mocking interfaces is straightforward.: Structs can evolve without breaking changes.: Consumers get full access to the returned type’s capabilities.By mirroring the standard library’s approach, your code will feel idiomatic and integrate smoothly with Go’s ecosystem.: Explore this Medium article for additional perspectives on this principle.
Final Thoughts: Make Your APIs Go-idiomatic The accept interfaces, return structs principle is more than a guideline—it’s a philosophy that shapes robust, flexible, and testable Go APIs. By studying the standard library and applying these practices, you’ll write code that’s not only functional but also a joy to use. Next time you design a Go API, ask yourself: Am I accepting interfaces for flexibility and returning structs for power? If so, you’re on the right track.

item.title: Effective Design Patterns in Go
item.author: Leapcell
item.content:encoded:
Ensure that only one instance of a class is created globally, and provide a unified access point. It belongs to the creational pattern and is suitable for scenarios where global unique control is required.: There is only one object instance globally.: The class itself is responsible for creating the instance.: Provide an access entry through a static method or a global variable.
Advantages and Disadvantages Ensure global uniqueness of resources/stateViolate the principle of high cohesion, with high module couplingReduce repeated creation of resourcesDifficult to isolate unit testsProvide a convenient global access pointDamage the dependency injection patternMicroservice Configuration Center (such as an alternative to Spring Cloud Config): Manage the configuration of distributed systems uniformly. (such as connection management for PostgreSQL/MySQL): Control the number of concurrent connections. (such as the log collector in the ELK Stack): Avoid repeated creation of log handlers. (such as the request frequency control of the Stripe API): Share the rate limiting state globally.
Go Language Implementation (Concurrency-safe Version) Encapsulate the object creation logic, and determine the specific product to be instantiated through subclasses. It belongs to the creational pattern and decouples the object creation from its usage.Encapsulation of Creation Logic: The client does not need to know the specific creation details.: Extend the product family through interfaces.: Adding new products does not require modifying the existing code.
Advantages and Disadvantages Separate object creation from usageIncrease the number of classes (each product needs a corresponding factory)Easy to extend new productsThe factory class may become too complexComply with the Dependency Inversion PrincipleThe client needs to know the abstract interface of the productPayment Gateway Integration (Stripe/PayPal/Apple Pay): Create different processors according to the payment method. (S3/GCS/Azure Blob): Create corresponding API clients according to the storage service. (Kafka/RabbitMQ/NATS): Unify the creation logic of the message sending interface.Third-party Login Service (OAuth2/OpenID Connect): Dynamically generate authentication clients for different platforms.
Go Language Implementation (Abstract Factory Pattern) Define a one-to-many dependency relationship between objects, and automatically notify all observers when the state of the subject changes. It belongs to the behavioral pattern and is suitable for event-driven scenarios.: The change of the subject's state triggers the update of the observers.: The subject and the observers only interact through abstract interfaces.: Observers can register or unregister at any time.
Advantages and Disadvantages Support broadcast-style event notificationA large number of observers may lead to performance problemsEasy to expand new observer typesCircular dependencies may cause memory leaksComply with the Open-Closed PrincipleThe subject needs to maintain a list of observersReal-time Notification System (Slack channel updates/Zoom meeting reminders): Users subscribe to the subject to receive real-time notifications. (Robinhood/Nasdaq real-time quotes): Investors subscribe to stock codes to get price changes.E-commerce Inventory Monitoring (Amazon product restocking notifications): Users subscribe to products to receive inventory change reminders.Distributed System Monitoring (Datadog metric alarms): The monitoring service subscribes to the state changes of microservices.
Go Language Implementation (Event-driven Model) Dynamically add new functions to an object, and extend its responsibilities through composition rather than inheritance. It belongs to the structural pattern and is suitable for scenarios with functional layering.: Keep the interface consistent, and the client is unaware of it.: Support nesting of multiple layers of decorators.: Dynamically determine the combination of object functions.
Advantages and Disadvantages Avoid inheritance hierarchy explosionDebugging may be difficult with multiple layers of decoratorsSupport free combination of functionsExcessive use will increase the complexity of the systemComply with the Open-Closed PrincipleThe order of decoration may affect the execution result (Express.js/Koa.js middleware system): Stack functions such as logging, authentication, and rate limiting.E-commerce Order Processing (shipping fee calculation/discount offer/tax processing): Calculate the total order amount step by step. (S3 encryption/compression/CDN acceleration): Dynamically combine storage functions. (Kong/NginX plugin system): Request verification, response transformation, and traffic monitoring.
Go Language Implementation (HTTP Middleware Simulation) Encapsulate algorithms into independent strategy classes, and support dynamic switching at runtime. It belongs to the behavioral pattern and is suitable for scenarios with multiple algorithms.: Each strategy independently implements the algorithm.: Dynamically select a strategy at runtime.: Adding a new strategy does not require modifying the existing code.
Advantages and Disadvantages Decouple the algorithm from the clientThe client needs to know all strategy typesFacilitate algorithm extension and replacementThe number of strategy classes may grow rapidlySupport combined strategiesIt is difficult to share the state between strategiesE-commerce Promotion Engine (full reduction/discount/gift strategies): Dynamically switch the calculation logic according to the activity type.Logistics Distribution System (FedEx/UPS/USPS shipping strategies): Calculate the shipping fee according to the user's choice. (sort by price/sales volume/rating): The front-end request determines the sorting strategy.Advertisement Placement Algorithm (precision marketing/geographical targeting/frequency control): Adjust the placement strategy in real time.
Go Language Implementation (E-commerce Promotion Calculation) Convert incompatible interfaces so that classes with different interfaces can work together. It belongs to the structural pattern and is suitable for system integration scenarios.: Adapt the source interface to the target interface.: The client and the adaptee do not need to interact directly.: Retain the original system code and add an adapter layer.
Advantages and Disadvantages Solve the problem of interface incompatibilityIncrease the complexity of the system's abstract layerExcessive use may make the system difficult to maintainSupport two-way adaptationMay introduce performance overhead (Docking a COBOL system with a microservice architecture): Adapt the old system API.Third-party Library Integration (Adapt the MongoDB driver to a SQL interface): Unify the data access layer.Cross-platform Development (Adapt iOS/Android native APIs to a unified interface): Mobile application framework. (Adapt AWS SQS to the Kafka message format): Hybrid cloud architecture.
Go Language Implementation (Third-party Payment Adaptation) Provide a proxy layer for the target object to control access to it. It belongs to the structural pattern and is suitable for scenarios such as remote access and permission control.: The proxy layer intercepts and processes requests.: Create the target object on demand.Separation of Responsibilities: The proxy handles non-business logic (logging/security/caching).
Advantages and Disadvantages Protect the target objectIncrease the request processing delaySupport distributed accessThe proxy layer may become a single point of failureThe interfaces of the proxy class and the target class need to be consistent (Apigee/Postman API management): Proxy backend microservices and provide a unified entry. (Redis/Memcached cache layer): Cache database query results. (OAuth2 authorization server): Intercept requests and verify user permissions. (gRPC remote service call): Hide the details of network communication.
Go Language Implementation (Cache Proxy) Encapsulate requests into command objects to decouple the request sender from the receiver. It belongs to the behavioral pattern and is suitable for scenarios that support undo and logging.: The command object contains all the information required for execution.: Support batch execution and rollback.: The execution history of commands can be persisted.
Advantages and Disadvantages Support undo/redo operationsThe number of command classes may increase significantlyDecouple request sending and receivingComplex command logic is difficult to maintainCommand parameters need to be determined in advance (Git commit operations): Each commit can be treated as a command that supports rollback. (Encapsulation of ACID operations): Combine multiple SQL commands to support commit/rollback. (Airflow task orchestration): Encapsulate distributed tasks into executable commands. (Undo/Redo functions): Record editing operations as command objects.
Go Language Implementation (Database Transaction Simulation) Combine objects into a tree structure and uniformly process individual objects (leaves) and composite objects (containers). It belongs to the structural pattern and is suitable for hierarchical management scenarios.: Support part-whole hierarchical relationships.: The client treats leaves and containers consistently.: Container objects can contain other containers or leaves.
Advantages and Disadvantages Simplify hierarchical structure operationsSupport complex hierarchical traversalThe interfaces of leaves and containers need to be strictly unifiedComply with the Open-Closed PrincipleFunction expansion may affect the overall structure (AWS S3's Bucket/Object structure): Process files and folders uniformly. (Department/Employee management in an enterprise HR system): A department can contain sub-departments and employees.E-commerce Product Catalog (Amazon's category/subcategory/product hierarchy): Hierarchical product management. (View/Text component nesting in React Native): Process the UI component tree uniformly.
Go Language Implementation (File System Simulation) Provide a unified interface to traverse the elements of a collection and hide the internal data structure of the collection. It belongs to the behavioral pattern and is suitable for data traversal scenarios.: The client does not need to know the internal implementation of the collection.: Support traversal strategies such as forward, reverse, and filtering.: The collection and the iterator can evolve independently.
Advantages and Disadvantages Unify the collection access interfaceIncrease the design cost of the iterator classSupport traversal of complex data structuresCustomizing iterators may lead to class bloatComply with the Single Responsibility PrincipleThe iterator needs to maintain the traversal state (Traversal of data sets in Hadoop MapReduce): Process different storage formats uniformly. (Traversal of result sets in GORM): Transparently handle the return formats of different databases. (Traversal of scene graph nodes in Three.js): Process the hierarchical structure of 3D objects uniformly. (Traversal of log entries in ELK): Support traversal of different log storage formats.
Go Language Implementation (Custom Collection Iterator) The Go language provides flexible support for the implementation of design patterns through its interface and composition features: : Use the singleton pattern to control the global state, and the factory pattern to encapsulate object creation.: Achieve the functional extension of decorators, proxies, and adapters through composition.: Decouple and implement the interaction logic of observers, strategies, and commands with the help of interfaces.In actual development, an appropriate pattern should be selected according to specific scenarios: For microservice architectures, the factory pattern and the adapter pattern are preferred.For event-driven systems, the observer pattern and the command pattern are recommended.For hierarchical data management, the composite pattern and the iterator pattern are the first choices.The reasonable use of design patterns can improve the maintainability, extensibility, and reusability of the code. However, over-design should be avoided, and the focus should always be on solving practical problems.Finally, I would like to recommend a platform that is most suitable for deploying Go services.
🚀 Build with Your Favorite Language Develop effortlessly in JavaScript, Python, Go, or Rust.
🌍 Deploy Unlimited Projects for Free Only pay for what you use—no requests, no charges.
⚡ Pay-as-You-Go, No Hidden Costs No idle fees, just seamless scalability.

item.title: Go for Node developers: creating an IDP from scratch - Set-up
item.author: Afonso Barracha
item.content:encoded:
Nowadays, due to performance constraints a lot of companies are moving away from NodeJS to Go for their network and API stacks. This series is for developers interest in making the jump from Node.js to Go.On this series, I will teach the process of building an IDP (identity provider), a project I started to create a comunity driven self-hosted opensource version of clerk.This series requires some basic knowledge of Go, as it will teach you how to structure a Go API, and how to transfer Node.js to Go skills. Before starting this series, I recommend reading the following two books to gain familiarity with the Go programming language:Or if you're in a hurry just take a tour of Go in the official website.This series will be divided in 6 parts:Project structure and Database design: I'll lay the foundation by setting up the project structure and designing the database schema for the IDP.Local and External Authentication with JWTs: I will touch on how I implemented local authentication using JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) Bearer tokens (RFC-6750) for our Accounts (tenants) including external auth (RFC-6749). to send emails we will create our own queue using redis and the package from the standard library.Component testing with docker-compose: I'll touch on how to ensure the reliability of our API by writing endpoint tests using Docker Compose and Go's standard and packages.: add multiple apps support for each account. We will deploy our App to a VPS (virtual private server) using coolify.A well-organized project structure is a requirement for building maintainable and scalable APIs. In this article, we'll explore how to structure a Go API using a very common pattern in the Node.JS world, using the Model, Service, Controller (MSC) architecture.We will leverage the following stack: SQLC is a Go code generator with type safety and compile-time checks for our SQL queries.Redis with fiber's Redis abstraction: a caching storage abstraction for fiber using Redis.We will also design the database schema and create the necessary migrations to set up our data layer.For the structure of our code we'll levarage the go-blueprint CLI tool made by the youtuber Melky.To start the project install and initialize the project:go github.com/melkeydev/go-blueprint@latest makedir devlogs devlogs go-blueprint create idp fiber postgres commit This will lead to an initial file structure like:C:/Users/user/IdeProjects/devlogs/idp: ├─── cmd │ ├─── api │ │ main.go ├─── internal │ ├─── database │ │ database.go │ ├─── server │ │ server.go │ │ routes.go │ │ routes_test.go | ... While the name of the module will be , this is a bit far of what we want, which would be github.com/your-username/devlogs/idp. So update the name to github.com/your-username/devlogs/idp and run:And move the files around for a MSC (Model, Service, Controller) architecture:C:/Users/user/IdeProjects/devlogs/idp: ├─── cmd │ ├─── api │ │ main.go ├─── internal │ ├─── config │ │ config.go │ │ encryption.go │ │ logger.go │ │ oauth.go │ │ rate_limiter.go │ │ tokens.go │ │ ... │ ├─── controllers │ │ ├─── bodies │ │ │ common.go │ │ │ ... │ │ ├─── params │ │ │ common.go │ │ │ ... │ │ ├─── paths │ │ │ common.go │ │ │ ... │ │ auth.go │ │ controllers.go │ │ ... │ ├─── exceptions │ │ controllers.go │ │ services.go │ ├─── providers │ │ ├─── cache │ │ │ cache.go │ │ │ ... │ │ ├─── database │ │ │ database.go │ │ │ ... │ │ ├─── mailer │ │ │ mailer.go │ │ │ ... │ │ ├─── oauth │ │ │ oauth.go │ │ │ ... │ │ ├─── tokens │ │ │ tokens.go │ │ │ ... │ │ ├─── encryption │ │ │ encryption.go │ │ │ ... │ ├─── database │ │ database.go │ ├─── server │ │ ├─── routes │ │ │ routes.go │ │ │ ... │ │ logger.go │ │ server.go │ │ routes.go │ ├─── services │ │ ├─── dtos │ │ │ common.go │ │ │ ... │ ├─── utils │ │ ... | ... Most logic will live on the folder, where the main structure is as follows: centralizes and loads all environment configurations. contains the Fiber instance initialization and endpoints routing
: specifies the routes for a given controller method.: builds the default configuration for the structure logger.: has the main method to register all routes .: builds a instance with the instance and routes router. handle incoming HTTP requests, process them using services, and return the appropriate HTTP responses.
: specifies the controllers bodies.: specifies the controllers URL and Query parameters.: where the routes path constants are defined so they can be easily shared. where most of our business logic and interactions with external providers lives.
: where our data transfer objects are defined, where we have our external providers such as Data stores, JWTs, etc.
: Redis storage interactions.: PostgreSQL interactions and model structs implementations.: connection to our mailing queue.: oauth interactions with external authentication providers.: jwt signing and verifying.As most APIs, the configuration will come from environment variables, we can load them with the package from the standard library.For local development we can load the environment variables from a file, by installing the following package:go get github.com/joho/godotenv This variables most of the time act as secrets, hence we will use a OOP style encapsulation with them where all members of configurations structs are private and immutable, while you can get their values with getters.Our IDP will have main configurations groups apart from the base : whether debug is active and the env to chose whether we want a text or JSON handler. the private/public keys pairs and TTL for signing and verifying JWTs. collection of client ids and secrets for each of the external authentication providers. specifies the max number of request withing a window that an IP can make. list of KEK (Key encryption keys) provided to the environment.On the directory create a file with the following struct and function builder: for most methods in Go it is recommended using pointer receivers. Pointers are just address pointing to the underlying memory of the struct, but for simplicity you can think of them as pass by reference instead of pass by value.JWT have 3 main parts that need to be provided by the environment: the key that will be used to verify the token. the key that will be used to sign the token. the TTL in seconds of the token.And the service will have 7 different JWTs with different key pairs: for the access token. for machine to machine access. for the refresh token to refresh the access token on client to machine access. for the email confirmation token (JWT for confirmation can be overkill, however it saves on ram memory by not saving the hashed email in cache). for email resetting. for a temporary authorization header for the token exchange. for a temporary authorization header for passing the two factor code.Create a file on the directory with the struct, new method and getters:Now do the same as previously but for each token type:External authentication providers have two environment variables each: the identifier of the app on the external IDP a secure secret to fetch the user info from the code exchange.And we will add support for the 5 main western ones:
Single OAuth provider configuration Create a file on the directory with the struct, new method and getters:
OAuth providers configuration Create a struct, new method and getter for each provider:The rate limiter has only two options: the number of maximum number of requests per window size. the window size in seconds.Just create the file on the directory as follows:Finaly to configure the KEKs for each encryption space:: encryption of secrets for TOTP two factor auth.: encryption for the APPs private keys.: encryption of secrets for TOTP two factor auth.As well as old keys for easy keys rotation.Add them to file:On the base config you just need to add the environment variables in an array, as well as getters for each config parameter of the struct:With the config done, we can start creating the providers: PostgreSQL database; Redis PubSub publisher for emails (the queue will be built on a subsquent article); external authentication providers; jwt signing and verifying; the provider for envolope encryption (more of an isolation of logic than a proper provider. Encryption logic should always be isolated in production)Observasibility of APIs is important for production debugging, so lets create an utility to build a structure logger.To make it easy to locate the function and logic, we need to pass the package location, method, and layer. Create the utility on the root package. Create a file and add the following code:Our cache implementation will just extend the fiber Redis abstraction. On the create a directory and add file with the following struct and new function:With three common methods to reset the cache, get the redist client and ping the cache:The database set-up is a bit more complex. We will write both our migrations and queries in SQL and use migrate to migrate our DB changes, and SQLC to generate safe Go SQL query codes.Start by installing the necessary dependencies:go github.com/sqlc-dev/sqlc/cmd/sqlc@latest go github.com/golang-migrate/migrate/v4/cmd/migrate@latest And create a config file for SQLC as on the root folder:Since we are creating an IDP we will have three main entitites: (also known as tenants): the main users/admins that can create APPs that provide authentication to their own services.: the authentication provider configuration for a given service or services.: users that have registered for the 's s.This leads to the following somewhat complex datamodel:With migrate installed run the following command:migrate create sql ./internal/providers/database/migration create_initial_schema This will generate two files:YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_create_initial_schema.up.sqlYYYYMMDDHHMMSS_create_initial_schema.down.sqlTo the up migration copy the following SQL:While on the down drop all tables if they exist:To set up the SQLC generated code we need to start by writting a query on the folder. Create a file in the directory and add the logic to insert an account:SQLC knows what to return and what to call to the go method by the code comment on top of the SQL code.Then in the terminal run the following command:This will generate the following files:These files are auto-generated and auto-updated when you run the command, hence on a new create the logic to connect to the SQLC generated code:Encryption is less of a provider, but more of a logic isolation, hence we'll still load it as a provider.For each secret (or key), we will need to derive a KID (Key ID), this is common logic, so in the directory lets create a package and on a folder add the following files:Now with the utilites done, create the following package:DEKs (Date Encryption Keys) need to be generated by the system, and are saved in the database, for this reason, create a file called and add the following logic:DEK generation & Encryption: Methods to decrypt for Account, App & User: The mailer will just be a redis publisher, that will publish messages to the email service, hence create a package with the following file:It will consume the from the cache, and publish to a given channel.For OAuth 2.0 we can just use the package, just run:Each external provider has their own scopes, lets start by creating a scopes struct:And for each provider lets create a global scope variable:Start by creating an struct for each external provider, using existing defaults when possible:For getting the token we will need to pass the correct Scopes, this touches on a concept in go that is passing a value as a value (or copy) of the underlying resource. Since we dynamically add scopes we create a copy of the config each time.Getting the access token:Getting the authorization URL:Common options between all providers:When choosing the algorithm of the key pairs to sign and verify JWTs we need to choose the best ones with the best efficient and security ratio in mind.For sharing and saving keys we need to convert them to JWKs so create an encode and decode functions for both Ed25519 and P256 on the file:For each key we need a key pair, and a reference to a previous public key for keys rotation, create the package:Now each key is encoded as a PEM in the environment, hence we need to decode the x509 certificates for:Finally finish by creating the full provider:Error handling in Go works differently from most languages, instead of throwing and catching exceptions, values are return as a pointer (error as values) to the error.This means for ease of use, we need to map/coerce the errors to a known value on the service layer, and map them to an error response on the controller layer.Create an directory on the folder for the errors mapping.The service error is gonna have a standard error class, with a type/code, and a message, but for simplicity we won't add a details slice field.Create the file for the package:We need to standerdize the and based on HTTP status codes:For the PostgreSQL errors we can coerce them using a mapper: need to be mappend to a JSON Response, however there are also other types of error responses: body validation and oauth validation.Create the and add the error response, which is the same as but JSON parsable:To validation the body inputs we will use the go validator package, start by installing it:go get github.com/go-playground/validator/v10 Services is where most of the server business logic is located, lets create the package, the struct needs to encapsulate all providers, and be the only layer talking directly to them.We also need a helper to build the logger, and some common constant, create a file:For our first service, we will create the health endpoint for our API, this service only needs to ping PostgreSQL and ValKey.Controllers are where we map our services to the correct HTTP status response.For the controllor, error handling and logging is the same for all routes, hence create a logger builder and error handling functions on a file.The health controller is pretty simple, since it will only consume the service method directly:On the controllers folder create a paths package with the path:The server is where we will initialize the Fiber App, build Services, Controllers and load common middleware.Start by creating a and add file with the struct:Now create a router method for the health endpoint on :Plus also create a with the route for API version 1:Server instance is where we hook up most of our logic, hence it will be a big method where you take the config as a parameter and initialize everything.On the file inside the directory, and create the instance:And load everything in the function that takes the , the struter logger () and the configuration ():Just create a file inside the directory with a method:For logging we will need to create a default logger initially then add the configuration when they are loaded on startup, on a file:On the folder's file just set-up everything and register the routes:In this first article, we introduced the fiber framework, a express inspired go library, as well as how to set up our API with a MSC (model, service, controller) architecture with a centralized configuration.This code is based on the current ongoing project found on the devlogs repository.Hey there! I am Afonso Barracha, your go-to econometrician who found his way into the world of back-end development with a soft spot for GraphQL. If you enjoyed reading this article, why not show some love by buying me a coffee?Lately, I have been diving deep into more advanced subjects. As a result, I have switched from sharing my thoughts every week to posting once or twice a month. This way, I can make sure to bring you the highest quality content possible.Do not miss out on any of my latest articles – follow me here on dev, LinkedIn or Instagram to stay updated. I would be thrilled to welcome you to our ever-growing community! See you around!

item.title: Go + WASM: Run Bytecode Code in Your Browser
item.author: Athreya aka Maneshwar
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking "Can I run native binaries in the browser?" Naturally, my mind jumped to languages like Go or Rust—languages built compiling down to something close to the metal (bin). But here's the twist: browsers don't speak Go or Rust.They speak JavaScript. Or at least, they used to.This is where WebAssembly comes in.WebAssembly (or WASM) is like a virtual CPU that runs inside your browser. It defines its own instruction set. It doesn't compile to x86 or ARM, but to its own binary format that the browser can understand and execute efficiently.It’s kind of like Java’s JVM bytecode—but made for performance and for modern compilers. And unlike Java, it wasn’t designed in the 90s.WebAssembly is fast. Like, really fast. Not "a little faster than JS"—but fast. That means you can take a performance-critical app (say, image processing, or running emulators) and run it in the browser .The people behind WASM knew what they were doing. The spec was designed by folks who deeply understand compiler and CPU internals. So even though it’s portable and sandboxed, it can still get compiled to native CPU instructions quickly and efficiently.
Wait, Is It Like Assembly? WASM borrows a lot of ideas from traditional assembly:It’s imperative: instructions run one after another.It has a stack: push values, pop them off, compute.It has branching, memory access, arithmetic—all the usual suspects.But it’s not tied to any real CPU. It’s not x86, not ARM, not RISC-V. It’s WASM’s own thing. That’s why you can compile languages like Rust, C/C++, or Go into WASM. These compilers already know how to emit instructions for different architectures—and WASM is just one more "target" for them.
Is It Only for Compiling Native Code? Mostly, yes. That’s the main use case right now: compiling existing codebases written in C/C++/Rust/Go into something that runs in the browser.But there's also (WebAssembly System Interface), which is trying to bring system-level APIs to WASM—so you can run WASM outside the browser, too.That’s where things get interesting. Now you can run sandboxed WASM modules on the server, inside a CLI tool, or as plugins. Think Docker containers, but even more lightweight.
What Can You Build With It? Here’s where it gets fun. WebAssembly opens up a whole new set of use cases for the web (and beyond):: things like FFmpeg or Audacity in the browser: NES, SNES, PS1 emulators running natively in JS environments: apps that allow user-contributed code (think OBS or VS Code extensions): compile once, run everywhere—desktop, mobile, browserAnd if you're doing backend stuff, some platforms (like Wasmer or Wasmtime) let you run WASM on the server. That means you can build multi-tenant systems where user code runs isolated and safely.go-wasm ├─ Makefile ├─ client.html ├─ go.mod ├─ main.go ├─ main.wasm └─ wasm_exec.js
2. : Export Go Function to JavaScript
3. : Build and Init js wasm go build main.wasm
find /usr/local/go wasm_exec.js | 1 ./wasm_exec.js : Compiles the Go code to .: Copies from Go's installation path (required to run WASM in browser). And boom—your Go binary is now browser-ready.
4. : Browser UI + JS Glue Go WASM Character CounterGo WASM Character CounterCount CharactersYou need to serve over HTTP (not ) because WASM loading via needs a web server.python3 http.server 8080
npx serve Open http://localhost:8080/client.html in your browser.To tie it back—yes, you can compile Go code to WebAssembly. The Go compiler has built-in support for . You just write Go like normal, export a function, and run it in the browser with a little JavaScript glue.So yeah—binaries in the browser? Turns out, it’s not just possible. It’s actually kind of awesome.I’ve been actively working on a super-convenient tool called LiveAPI.LiveAPI helps you get all your backend APIs documented in a few minutesWith LiveAPI, you can quickly generate interactive API documentation that allows users to execute APIs directly from the browser.If you’re tired of manually creating docs for your APIs, this tool might just make your life easier.

item.title: Method Receivers in Go: Demystifying Value and Pointer Receivers
item.author: Shrijith Venkatramana
item.content:encoded:
Hi there! I'm Shrijith Venkatrama, founder of Hexmos. Right now, I’m building LiveAPI, a tool that makes generating API docs from your code ridiculously easy.Go doesn't have traditional classes or inheritance, but it does let you associate methods with types. This happens through . If you've ever wondered whether to use a value or a pointer receiver in Go, this is the detailed, no-nonsense guide for you.
What Are Method Receivers in Go? A method receiver in Go is the value or pointer on which a method operates. It is defined like so:This makes a method tied to . Go doesn't have classes, but this is how you associate behavior with data.Unlike Java or C++, Go makes the receiver explicit, and it can either be a value or a pointer.
Two Kinds of Receivers: Value and Pointer Go supports two types of method receivers:
Value Receivers: Call by Value Copies the struct when the method is called.Cannot modify the original struct.Preferred for small, immutable-like types.You don't need to modify fields.You want consistent behavior (immutability-style).
Pointer Receivers: Reference-Like Semantics Operates on the original struct.Can modify the receiver's internal state.Can be more efficient for large structs.You want to modify the receiver.The struct is large or contains reference-heavy fields.You already use pointer receivers elsewhere on the type.
Method Sets and Their Role Go defines what methods a type has through :For : includes all methods with .For : includes all methods with pointer or value receivers.This impacts whether a type can satisfy an interface.
How Go Handles Calls: Implicit Conversions Go is flexible when it comes to calling methods:When calling , Go will auto-take the address if needed — but only if the method has a pointer receiver.
Real Performance Implications Using a value receiver here would trigger a full copy on each method call. Prefer pointer receivers when working with large data types or when mutation is required.
Consistency Rule: Avoid Mixing Receiver Types If one method on a type uses a pointer receiver, all methods should. This avoids:Confusion about behavior.Interface method mismatch.Implicit address/dereference bugs.
Receiver Behavior with Maps, Slices, Channels Value receivers do not protect against mutation if fields themselves are reference types:Even though is a value receiver, the underlying slice may be shared and mutated.
Interface Compatibility and Traps You define an interface that expects a method with pointer receiver:You must use the pointer if the method is defined on .
Summary: Choosing the Right Receiver Needs to modify receiver?All methods should match?If method needs a pointer receiver, make all methods use pointer receivers.Keep behavior and memory usage predictable.Got questions or deeper edge cases? Leave a comment. This topic is subtle but critical to mastering Go.
