item.title: **Health Journalism: Combating Misinformation with Accuracy
item.author: Turacoz Group
item.content:encoded:
By **Turacoz Healthcare Solutions (https://turacoz.com/)* | World Liver Day 2025* (https://turacoz.com/health-journalism-combating-misinformation-with-accuracy/) In a world where social media dominates wellness discussions, liver health is a popular topic — albeit not always a well-informed one. From miracle cures to detox diets, the liver is the focus of myriad health claims with little scientific basis. On this World Liver Day, we highlight the vital role of health journalism in separating fact from fiction and delivering accurate, science-based information. **Emergence of Misinformation about Liver Health Digital media have opened health information to everyone—but with such openness comes an influx of unfiltered information. Wellness bloggers, often with little to no medical training, tout liver ‘cleanses’ and ‘superfoods’ without accountability. These messages, though alluring, can mislead consumers, undermine patient education campaigns, and jeopardize liver health. False information not only causes confusion but also can delay treatment and accurate diagnosis. With early treatment being essential in conditions such as non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), hepatitis, and cirrhosis, patients are at a loss if discussion centers around misinformation.The Role of Health Journalism in Public Health Evidence-based health journalism acts as a link between health practitioners and the general population. Accurate, accessible, and understandable health reporting translates complex medical jargon into clear messages, enhancing health literacy and supporting informed decision-making. Medical writers and journalists have an obligation to:Confirm information from evidence-based sources like peer-reviewed journals and clinical practice guidelines.Work with specialists such as hepatologists, nutritionists, and scientists.Employ health information management systems to track, interpret, and share accurate data. Fact-checking in the Social Media era With misinformation traveling at lightning speed on the internet, fact-checking now forms a bedrock of trustworthy health communication. Using health information technology like automated content verification systems and AI-based surveillance, medical communicators can quickly address misleading claims and disseminate accurate content to the masses. Agencies, along with journalists, should also predict patterns. Tracking social media discussions enables them to correct misleading reports in a timely fashion and present scientifically correct counter-information. Empowering Patients Through Education Correct health information not only educates, it empowers. Liver health education should be centered in useful, actionable information: comprehending liver function, identifying risks, adopting evidence-based dietary practices, and recognizing warning signs that necessitate medical care. By ensuring that educational resources are synchronized with national health goals and governmental standards, medical communicators contribute effectively to national campaigns promoting liver health. A Partnership with Health Professionals Effective health journalism does not exist in a vacuum. It is developed in close collaboration with health professionals, patient advocacy organizations, and public health organizations. Such collaborations help guarantee that information is up to date, applicable, and consistent with clinical best practices.
Combating misinformation on matters related to the liver necessitates a multifaceted approach:
Being a trusted medical communications agency, Turacoz is of the opinion that science-based, accessible, and honest communication is central to improved health outcomes. On this World Liver Day, we reaffirm our values of truth, science, and service—because timely information is a matter of saving people’s lives. info@turacoz.com (mailto:info@turacoz.com) Singapore | India | Netherlands

item.title: Parsing and Formatting Time Strings 5/10
item.author: Rez Moss
item.content:encoded:
Working with dates and times is a fundamental part of programming, yet it can be surprisingly tricky. Go takes a unique approach to time formatting and parsing that might seem strange at first but becomes quite elegant once you understand it. In this article, I'll walk you through how Go handles time strings, from formatting to parsing, and cover some common pitfalls you might encounter along the way.
How Go formats dates with "2006-01-02 15:04:05" If you're coming from other programming languages, Go's approach to date formatting might throw you off initially. Instead of using format specifiers like or , Go uses a reference date: January 2, 2006 at 3:04:05 PM MST.This reference date isn't arbitrary. It's actually a clever mnemonic: 2006 (year), 01 (month), 02 (day), 15 (hour), 04 (minute), 05 (second). Or, numerically: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 (where 7 corresponds to the time zone, MST or -0700).The beauty of this approach is that your format string looks exactly like the output you want. Want your date to look like "Jan 02, 2006"? That's exactly what you use as your format string. No need to remember that means abbreviated month name or that means 4-digit year.The reference time also includes the following components that you can use:Year: 2006 Month: 01 (numeric) or Jan (name) Day: 02 (numeric) or Monday (name) Hour: 15 (24-hour) or 3 (12-hour) Minute: 04 Second: 05 Timezone: MST or -0700 The reference date is also known as the "magic date" or the "magic constant" among Go developers. Once you get used to it, you'll likely find it more intuitive than the format specifiers used in other languages.
Formatting time values with Once you understand Go's reference date approach, using the method becomes straightforward. This method takes a layout string as its parameter and returns a formatted string representation of the time value.You can mix and match the reference date components to create virtually any date format you need. Here are some more examples:Go also provides some predefined layouts in the package for common formats:The method always returns a string, so it's perfect for situations where you need to display dates to users or include them in logs, JSON responses, or any other text-based output.One thing to keep in mind is that the method uses the time zone information stored in the time value. If your time value doesn't have the correct time zone set, your formatted output might not be what you expect. We'll cover time zones in more detail in a later section.
Parsing time strings into (, ) Parsing is the inverse of formatting—it converts string representations of dates and times into values. Go provides several functions for this purpose, with the two most common being and .
Basic Parsing with The function takes two parameters: a layout string and the time string to parse. The layout string should follow the same reference date format we discussed earlier.One important detail to note: returns a value in UTC by default, unless the input string explicitly specifies a time zone. This is a common source of confusion for newcomers to Go.
Parsing with Time Zones using If you're parsing a time string that doesn't include time zone information but you know what time zone it should be in, use . This function takes an additional parameter:Using ensures that the parsed time is correctly interpreted in the specified time zone, which can be crucial for applications dealing with users in different parts of the world.
Working with Unix Timestamps For working with Unix timestamps (seconds since January 1, 1970 UTC), Go provides the function:This is particularly useful when working with systems that represent time as Unix timestamps, such as many databases and APIs.Remember that parsing operations can fail if the input string doesn't match the expected format. Always check the error return value from parsing functions to ensure your code handles invalid input gracefully.
Handling different formats and time zones Time zones can be one of the most challenging aspects of working with dates and times in any programming language, and Go is no exception. Let's explore how to handle different formats and time zones effectively.
Working with Multiple Formats In real-world applications, you often need to parse dates in various formats. Here's how you can handle multiple potential formats:This function tries each format in sequence and returns the first successful parse. Note that this is a naive implementation and might not work correctly for ambiguous date formats (e.g., "01/02/2006" vs "02/01/2006").Time zones in Go are represented by the type. There are a few ways to get a :The function loads a location by its IANA Time Zone Database name (like "America/New_York" or "Asia/Tokyo"). These names are more reliable than abbreviations like "EST" or "JST" because they account for daylight saving time changes and historical time zone shifts.
Converting Between Time Zones Once you have a value, you can convert it to a different time zone using the method:It's important to understand that doesn't change the moment in time, just how it's represented. The underlying instant (like 2:30 PM UTC on March 19, 2025) remains the same, but it's expressed in terms of a different time zone.
Handling Time Zone in Format Strings You can include time zone information in your format strings:These formats are particularly useful when generating time strings that need to be consumed by other systems or when displaying times to users in different parts of the world.Remember that time zone handling can be complex, especially when dealing with daylight saving time transitions, historical time changes, and user inputs. Always test your code thoroughly with edge cases to ensure it behaves as expected.
Error handling in date parsing () Proper error handling is crucial when working with time parsing, as user input or external data might not always conform to your expected formats. Go's time package provides specific error types and patterns to help you deal with parsing failures gracefully.When a parsing operation fails, Go returns a that contains detailed information about what went wrong. This struct has the following fields:These fields help you identify exactly what part of the parsing failed:: The format string you provided: The input string you tried to parse: The specific layout element that failed to match: The corresponding part of the input string: A human-readable error messageYou can extract this information to provide more helpful feedback:This might output something like:Error parsing time: month out of range Layout: 2006-01-02, Value: 2025-13-45 Layout element: 01, Value element: 13
Practical Error Handling Strategies In practical applications, you'll want to implement more robust error handling patterns. Here are some strategies:
1. Validate Before Parsing For user-facing applications, it's often better to validate the format before attempting to parse:When parsing dates in non-critical contexts, you might want to provide a fallback:As we saw in the previous section, you can try multiple formats:
4. Logging and Monitoring In production systems, log parsing errors to help identify patterns of problematic input:After parsing, it's often a good idea to check if you got a zero value (), which might indicate a parsing problem even if no error was returned:By implementing thoughtful error handling for time parsing, you can make your applications more robust and provide better feedback to users, making the experience smoother even when things don't go exactly as planned.

item.title: Gerando Pagamentos via Pix com a AbacatePay
item.author: Wiliam V. Joaquim
item.content:encoded: Recentemente precisei integrar com gateways de pagamento via Pix, para um sistema que estou desenvolvendo, durante esse processo passei por alguns serviços como MercadoPago, Stripe, Pagar.me, entre outros, todos eles com uma burocracia e complexidade que não estava disposto a ter nesse momento, gostaria apenas de gerar um código pix de forma simples.Fazendo algumas pesquisas acabei conhecendo a AbacatePay uma solução recém criada, mas com a proposta de ser simples, dando uma olhada na sua documentação é realmente simples sua integração, e suas taxas são bem atrativas, com isso resolvi dar uma chance e testar, nesse novo post vou mostrar como fiz essa integração.Primeiramente, vamos usar Golang, é claro :)!Para exemplificar, vou deixar um print da estrutura do nosso projeto:Já abortei em outros posts sobre essa estrutura, você pode ver aquiO handler será responsável por receber as requisições validar os dados de entrada através de um dto e repassar para o service.: Será responsável por iniciar uma instância do handler e definir os metódos que precisamos implementar, vamos ter apenas dois métodos, uma para gerar o pix e outro para receber um webhook (vamos abordar sobre o webhhok).Com isso, precisamos implementar, mas antes vamos criar um validador para os dados de entrada da nossa aplicação, vamos criar dentro da pasta Validamos de forma bem simples a request, você pode aprimorar essa validação, como validar se é um email válido ou telefone.Com o pronto, vamos implementar o handler!Não irei me aprofundar muito em Go, mas de forma simples, tranformamos o na struct , depois chamamos o que criamos, por fim repassamos para o service que é onde vai conter nossa regra de negócio, o service terminando o processamento, repassamos a reposta ao usuário.Com o handler pronto, precisamos registrar as nossas rotas e para isso vamos criar dentro da pasta uma pasta e um arquivo Registramos duas rotas, uma para gerar o pix e outra para receber o webhook.O service vai ser responsável pela regra de negócio, será no service que vamos chamar a api da AbacatePay.Fica semelhante ao handler, mas agora o recebe um parâmetro que vai ser o client que vamos criar para chamar a api da AbacatePay.Antes de implementar os metódos do service, precisamos criar o chamando a api.Dentro da pasta vamos criar outra pasta chamada e um arquivo chamado :Nesse arquivo vamos ter o "contrutor" e os metódos que vamos precisar, vamos criar mais um para fins de exemplo que será o mas vamos usar apenas o .Repare que usamos o buscando de uma variável de ambiente, esse token deve ser adquirido na plataforma da abacatepay e adicionado no , vou deixar tudo isso no repositório do projeto.Também criei um apenas para melhorar os logs usando o logrus.Esse método é bem simples, apenas pegamos os dados que vamos receber, chamamos a api da AbacatePay e retornamos o que recebermos da api, isso já é suficiente para ter um pix pronto para o cliente efetuar o pagamento, simples não é?O não tem todos os dados que a api retorna, você pode conferir nas docs e adicionar.O método segue a mesma lógica, você pode ver no repositório.Com o http client criado, vamos implementar o service:Como nossa é regra é simples, o service ficou simples, fazemos apenas a chamada do abacatepayclient.GerarPix passando o que recebemos no dto, pegamos a resposta e repassamos ao usuários, retornamos apenas o , e , mas você pode retornar mais dados.Isso já nos retorna um id, esse id pode ser usado para consultar o status do pagamento, usando esse endpoint, e a abacate ainda fornece outro endpoint que você pode confirmar esse pagamento, veja aqui que bacana, basta informar o id que foi retornado e esse pagamento será confirmado e será enviado um webhook.Um webhook é uma forma de um sistema avisar outro automaticamente quando algo acontece. No nosso caso uma forma da AbacatePay nos informar que algo mudou, para isso precisamos registrar um endpoint do tipo que receba essa informação. Agora vamos implementear o do handler.Segue o mesmo padrão do validamos os dados e repassamos para o service, mas nesse caso temos que validar o esse secret é informado lá na AbacatePay ao criar um webhook, e será sempre enviado no query params, basta extrair e validar, por isso salvamos esse secrete em uma variável de ambiente ABACATEPAY_WEBHOOK_SECRET.Nosso service fica assim::Não vamos implementar aqui, pois isso varia conforme a regra de negócio, você pode enviar um e-mail para o usuário, atualizar algo no banco de dados. No exemplo recebemos o evento , poderiamos disparar um e-mail, liberar um acesso ao produto.Mas tudo isso leva um certo tempo, e nao podemos deixar a AbacatePay esperando esse processamento, alguns serviços exigem que o nosso endpoint que recebe o webhook responda em um tempo determinado, como 500 milisegundos por exemplo, disparar um e-mail, atualizar registro no banco, tudo isso faria que o tempo fosse atingido e o webhook seria marcado como não entregue, causando retentativas e possivelmente problemas (caso o sistema não esteja preparado para lidar com idempotência).Por isso é uma boa prática responder o mais rápido possível, você pode jogar isso para uma fila, com RabbitMQ, SQS, Kafka, e processar essa informação do webhook posteriormente.Mas, como estamos em desenvolvimento, não vamos receber esse webhook, pois ele precisa ser enviado para um endereço que esteja na web, nosso querido localhost não vale :)Pra resolver isso, podemos usar um túnel, que vai disponibilizar um endereço para nós e redirecionar o tráfego para nosso localhost por exemplo.Você pode usar o ngrok (é pago, mas é possível brincar com a versão free), aqui tem uma lista de várias soluções para tunelamento.No meu exemplo eu usei o zero trust da cloudflare, tem um limite free muito bom, a única desvantagem é que precisa adicionar um domínio a cloudflare.Caso use o ngrok, eles fornecerão um dominio aleatório, como https://399a-185-153-176-85.ngrok-free.app, portanto lá no painel da AbacatePay informe o webhook como https://399a-185-153-176-85.ngrok-free.app/webhook.Agora basta iniciar tudo no Iniciamos as rotas usando o go-chi para roteamento, depois criamos as instâncias do , e .ou se preferir com dockerCom isso nossa integração com a AbacatePay está rodando 🎉Embora a AbacatePay seja uma solução recente sua ,a sua a simplicidade me chamou a atenção, para quem busca uma integração ssem complicações com pix, pode ser uma ótima escolha, principalmente por sua taxa ser bem atrativa.Sempre olhe a documentação, a da AbacatePay é bem simples de entender.

item.title: 5 Go Interface Mistakes - And How to Avoid Them
item.author: Shrijith Venkatramana
item.content:encoded:
Hi there! I'm Shrijith Venkatrama, founder of Hexmos. Right now, I’m building LiveAPI, a tool that makes generating API docs from your code ridiculously easy.Interfaces in Go are a powerful feature, letting you define behavior without tying yourself to specific implementations. But they’re easy to misuse, leading to code that’s harder to maintain or understand. In this post, I’ll walk through five common Go interface mistakes, explain why they’re problematic, and show you how to fix them with . Each section includes a table summarizing the issue and fix, plus a link to relevant documentation where it makes sense. Let’s dive in.
1. Embedding Interfaces Without Clear Intent Embedding one interface into another can seem like a clean way to combine behaviors, but doing so without a clear purpose can and make them harder to implement. It can also lead to between interfaces, reducing flexibility.Here’s an interface that embeds another without clear justification:: Embedding in forces implementers to provide , even if the consumer only needs . This bloats the interface and reduces its reusability.Define interfaces and combine them only when necessary, or use composition explicitly in the consumer.: Keeping and separate allows consumers to use only the interfaces they need. can accept any type that satisfies or , improving flexibility.Embeds interfaces unnecessarilyDefines independent interfacesAllows selective use of interfacesObscures intent of interfaceClear purpose for each interface
2. Creating Overly Broad Interfaces Interfaces with too many methods are and . They violate the Interface Segregation Principle, forcing implementers to provide methods they don’t need.Here’s an interface with too many responsibilities:: If a function only needs to read items, the interface forces you to implement all methods, even unused ones like or .Split the interface into smaller, focused interfaces that serve specific purposes.: Smaller interfaces (, ) are easier to implement and test. You can combine them () when needed.Many methods, hard to implementFew methods, focused purposeRequires mocking all methodsAllows selective implementation
3. Exporting Interfaces with One Implementation Exporting an interface with only one implementation is often . It adds complexity without providing abstraction benefits, as there’s no polymorphism.Here’s an exported interface with a single implementation:: Since is the only implementation, the interface doesn’t add value. It just obscures the concrete type.Use the unless you expect multiple implementations.: Skipping the interface reduces code and makes the type’s behavior clear. If you need an interface later (e.g., for testing), define it where it’s consumed.Exported for single implementationUse concrete type directly
4. Overusing Empty Interfaces Using to accept any type sacrifices , leading to runtime errors and unclear code. It’s a last resort, not a default choice.Here’s a function using an empty interface:: While flexible, can’t enforce type constraints, and you might need type assertions for specific operations, risking panics.Use (introduced in Go 1.18) for type-safe flexibility.: Generics provide the flexibility of with compile-time type checking, reducing runtime errors.None, relies on assertionsCompile-time checks with genericsUnclear what types are expectedExplicit type constraintsErrors caught at compile time
5. Returning Interfaces Instead of Concrete Types Returning an interface from a function hides the , limiting what callers can do with the result. It also makes the API less predictable.Here’s a function returning an interface:: The caller can’t call because it’s not part of the interface, even though the underlying type supports it.Return the and let consumers define interfaces if needed.: Returning gives callers full access to its methods. Consumers can define interfaces like for specific needs.Concrete type provides full accessRestricts caller’s optionsAllows callers to define interfacesObscures implementation detailsGo interfaces are a lightweight way to define behavior, but they’re easy to overuse or misuse. By avoiding unnecessary embedding, , avoiding unnecessary exports, using generics over empty interfaces, and , you’ll write clearer, more maintainable Go code.Got a favorite Go interface tip or a mistake you’ve made? Drop it in the comments—I’d love to hear about it.Why is embedding interfaces without clear intent problematic? What principle do overly broad interfaces violate? Why is exporting an interface with one implementation redundant? What’s the main issue with using ? What happens when a function returns an interface instead of a concrete type? Bloats interfaces, reduces flexibility Interface Segregation Principle Adds complexity, no polymorphism

item.title: Don't Cry about Pointers anymore - Deep Dive with C, Go and Rust
item.author: Yashaswi Kumar Mishra
item.content:encoded:
Why TF does this blog even exist? Let’s be real — most of us treat pointers like radioactive waste. Let's ignore those dreadful stories of childhood when pointers were introduced to you like a BIG demon. But deep down, the truth is:Memory is power. And pointers? Pointers are your keys to the throne.Debugged a nasty segfault while chugging a huge load of coffee,Seen your app lag thanks to the garbage collector,Or tried passing huge structs around to make your code modular …Then you’ve already the need for pointers.This isn’t just a blog. This is a manifesto. We’re gonna break the myths, smash the fear, and teach you how to own memory like an absolute menace — across (just for understanding how pointers work), , and .By the end of this read, you’ll understand:Why they matter in modern backend systemsHow Go gives you a chill but powerful pointer experienceHow Rust forces you into greatness with ownership and borrowingLet’s cut the fluff and jump right in — welcome to the deep dive.
Basics: Pointers and How They Work (I'm Hoping Y'all Know C) A pointer just to another value. That's it. It is itself a variable that stores the address of another variable(this variable can be a pointer too). Let's see a quick code snippet in C to recap pointer basics.*p → Dereference the pointer (get the value it points to)&x → Address-of operator (get the memory address of x)int *p → Declare a pointer to an integerThis is where C shows you its true colors: dope performance, minimal overhead — but a single wrong move and it’s undefined behavior land. Manual memory management is both a blessing and a curse.Let's quickly take a look over other pointer shenanigans in C : Arrays decay into pointers — the name of an array is basically a pointer to its first element.A pointer to a pointer holds the address of another pointer. Useful in dynamic memory, linked lists, and certain APIs.Functions live in memory too, and you can point to them! That’s how callbacks and plugin systems are built.Generic pointers that can point to any type. But you need to cast them back before dereferencing.Wait! There is also pointer arithmetic in C.In C, pointers aren’t just addresses — they’re math-capable beasts. You can add or subtract integers to pointers to move across array elements (ptr + 1 points to the next element). But beware: mess up the math, and you're one *(ptr + 9999) away from summoning a segfault demon.(I swear ChatGPT wrote this!)I just skimmed through the surface of pointers in C. You definitely need to go into a lot more detail. If you want to work with pointers in C, burn this video into your head.In C, you need to manage memory manually using malloc or calloc and use the pointers very carefully and cautiously. While you have all the control, things can go south easily, even if you miss a little something. Before diving into how we handle pointer in Go or Rust, let us look at some common concepts in pointers.We already saw how to do this in C, and it is pretty easy in Go and Rust too. These code snippets will help you grasp it.
Dynamic Memory Allocation Even here, the code snippets will help you. The pointers are allocated memory in heap.
Stack vs Heap Allocation in Pointers When working with pointers in low-level languages like , , and , understanding how memory is allocated — either on the or the — is if you don’t want your programs to randomly explode (aka segfault).Fast and .Memory is allocated when a function is called and freed when it returns.Ideal for and .Returning a pointer to a is dangerous. Once the function exits, that memory is gone.Slower but gives (malloc/free in C, in Rust).Data lives until you explicitly free it (or a garbage collector does).Used for or data structures.Go doesn’t let you explicitly allocate on heap or stack — the based on escape analysis.Memory leaks, double freeStack = speed. Heap = flexibility. Misunderstanding either = pain.
Pass By Value v/s Pass By Reference A of the variable is passed to the function.The original variable .What is Pass-by-Reference?Pass-by-Value (default in C)Pass-by-Reference using PointerPass-by-Value (default in Go)Pass-by-Reference using PointersEven slices, maps, channels in Go are passed by , but since the value contains an internal pointer, they often behave like references.Rust always passes , but with () or (), you simulate reference behavior safely.Pass-by-Value (moves or copies the value)Pass-by-Reference (Borrowing)Rust ensures or using its and . (We will understand more of this later)
Key Facts and Gotchas — Pointers in C, Go, and Rust Cast between pointer typesCreate pointer-to-pointerManually manage heap (malloc/free)No safety — undefined behavior is easyManual memory management (malloc/free)Dangling pointers, memory leaks, buffer overflowsSegfaults due to wild pointersUse pointers to access and modify valuesPass pointers to functionsAllocate memory with or (for slices, maps, etc.)Create pointer-to-pointer () Manually free memory (garbage collected)Have nil-check safety for all types (you can nil dereference) Take pointer to literal ( is invalid)As said earlier, Go passes structs/slices by value, but since slices contain an internal pointer, they behave “reference-like.”Use references (, ) safelyUse , , for heap allocation and shared ownershipUse raw pointers in unsafe blocks (, )Use instead of Have null pointers ( is used instead)Mutably borrow more than once simultaneously (borrow checker)Dereference or do pointer arithmetic outside Leak memory unless you or
Garbage Collection in Go v/s Ownership in Rust Choose your weapon: vs .
Go's Garbage Collection (GC) — The Chill Roommate Go takes a comfy, high-level approach. You allocate memory, use it, and — the Go runtime’s GC cleans it up like a silent ninja.Go uses a concurrent, tri-color mark-and-sweep garbage collector.It pauses briefly, walks through live objects, marks them, and frees unmarked (unreachable) memory.GC runs in the — you don’t control it directly.Zero manual memory management.Great for productivity, safety, and fast dev cycles.No need to think about or memory leaks most of the time.GC pauses = potential latency spikes.GC can kick in at random times = non-deterministic behavior.Less control over workloads (e.g., real-time systems).GC is like Uber Eats: you chill, your trash gets picked up eventually — but not always when you want it.
Rust’s Ownership Model — The Memory Warlord’s Code “Why GC when you can just never make a memory mistake?”Instead of garbage collection, Rust uses ownership + lifetimes + borrowing to memory safety — . Every value has a single owner. You can lend references without transferring ownership. Ensures references are valid as long as they're used. Zero runtime cost (no GC!)Compile-time guarantees = , no data races.Absolute control — systems level performance, with high-level safety. (borrow checker will roast you alive at first).More verbose code with , , lifetimes.Need to think deeply about lifetimes and ownership flows.Rust is like being Batman: no sidekicks, no help — just you and your gear. But once you master it? You're unstoppable.
TL;DR — Go GC vs Rust Ownership Compile-time via OwnershipGC pauses, unpredictable latencyMostly safe, but nil pointers existGuaranteed via borrow checkerSteep learning curve, then powerIf you want with decent performance: .If you want with and are willing to tame the beast: .Want to be a ? Learn both. Use the right tool for the right kill.
Chaos to Calm - Pointers in Go So now that you've stared into the (pointer pun intended) with C — raw, dangerous, and obviously a double edged sword — let's take a deep breath and walk into a language that hands you pointers… but without the existential crisis.Go doesn’t give you pointer arithmetic. It won’t let you shoot yourself in the foot (most of the time). But it does give you:Direct memory referencing with & and *Lightweight object sharing via pointer receiversSafe heap allocation using new()And just enough low-level vibes to keep your inner villain happyBy the above examples , it must be pretty clear how pointers work in go, let's jump to working with structs and pointers in go.
Pointers and Structs in Go Structs in Go are value types by default. This means assigning a struct or passing it to a function copies the whole thing — which could suck for performance.
When to Use Pointers with Structs When your struct is and copying is expensiveWhen you want to mutate the original struct in a functionWhen you want to avoid frequent allocations in tight loops If your struct is small (e.g., a few fields) and used immutablyIf you don’t need to share/mutate state across functionsIf you're creating short-lived structs inside a loop — prefer values to help stack allocationIf it's less than or equal to 3 words (e.g., 24 bytes), pass by value.If you need shared/mutable state, use pointers.For methods, use pointer receivers if the method needs to mutate or avoid copying.
Memory Best Practices in Go Avoid unnecessary allocationsUse value receivers where mutation isn't needed.Preallocate slices and maps where possible.If a variable escapes to the heap (vs staying on the stack), it increases GC pressure.Use to check if variables escape.Use for reusable memoryGreat for reducing GC load in high-performance apps.They’re safe, controlled, and let you avoid copying large structs.In Go, everything has a zero value. Don’t waste allocations initializing something that’s already zero. ### ⚠️ What Go Doesn’t Let You Do (And That’s Okay)
No direct memory manipulation (you’ll need for that).But hey, that’s the point — Go protects you from footguns so you can ship faster without being reckless. You're still working with memory, just with guardrails.And there you have it — the journey from raw pointers to Go vs. Rust. We’ve covered how memory is managed at its most basic level, the stack vs. heap debate, and the power (and peril) of manual memory management.In the world of Rust, we’ve seen how the ownership system isn’t just a feature — it’s a whole new way to think about memory safety. With borrowing and lifetimes, Rust enforces a discipline that forces you to think ahead, leaving no room for memory leaks or dangling pointers. It’s a challenge, but one that builds robust, zero-cost abstractions.In Go, we saw how things are more laid-back with garbage collection and pointers that you only really need when performance or interop is key. Go’s approach has its benefits, but it doesn’t give you the same fine-grained control as Rust.Remember: pointers are a tool. Whether you’re dealing with them in Go or Rust, you control the power — but with great power comes great responsibility. Master these concepts, and you’ll be writing software that’s not just efficient, but invincible.Also, we’ve only scratched the surface with Rust. In the coming one, we’ll dive into the real villain territory of Rust’s ownership, smart pointers, lifetimes, threads, and channels. Are you ready to level up your backend skills?Until then, keep your pointers sharp and your memory clean.

item.title: Boosting Golang JSON Performance: 10 Proven Techniques for High-Speed Processing
item.author: Aarav Joshi
item.content:encoded:
As a best-selling author, I invite you to explore my books on Amazon. Don't forget to follow me on Medium and show your support. Thank you! Your support means the world! JSON processing is a critical component in many Golang applications, especially those dealing with web services, APIs, and data interchange. As applications scale, the standard encoding/json package can become a performance bottleneck. I've spent years optimizing JSON handling in high-throughput Go systems, and I'll share practical techniques to significantly improve performance.
Understanding the Performance Challenges The standard json package in Go relies heavily on reflection, which impacts performance. When marshaling or unmarshaling JSON, the package examines struct fields at runtime, causing overhead that compounds with data complexity and volume.This approach works well for simple cases but becomes inefficient when processing millions of records or working with large JSON documents.The first optimization step involves intelligent use of struct field tags.The option skips fields with zero values, reducing payload size. The hyphen tag () excludes fields from JSON processing entirely, and the suffix converts numeric types to strings, which can be more efficient for certain datasets.
Memory Allocation Optimization JSON processing often creates many temporary objects. Reducing allocations significantly improves performance.Using a buffer pool reduces garbage collection pressure and improves throughput by recycling objects.
Custom Marshaling Methods For performance-critical types, implementing custom MarshalJSON and UnmarshalJSON methods bypasses reflection entirely.This direct approach can be 2-5x faster than the standard marshaling process as it avoids reflection and minimizes allocations.
Streaming JSON Processing For large JSON documents, streaming processing prevents loading the entire structure into memory.This technique is ideal for processing large datasets or handling continuous data streams.
Code Generation for Maximum Performance For critical applications, code-generated JSON handlers provide the ultimate performance improvement. Libraries like easyjson, ffjson, and go-codec generate type-specific marshaling code.The generated code eliminates reflection entirely and optimizes for the specific struct layout, often offering 3-10x performance improvements.When dealing with large JSON documents but only needing a few fields, selective unmarshaling is valuable.This approach minimizes memory usage and processing time by focusing only on required fields.
Benchmarking Your JSON Processing Measuring performance is crucial before and after optimization.Run benchmarks with go test -bench=. -benchmem to evaluate both time and memory allocations.
Advanced Field Configurations Strategic field ordering can improve performance. Place commonly accessed fields first and organize fields to minimize padding.This approach reduces memory usage and improves cache locality.
Working with Map-Based JSON For dynamic JSON structures, using maps instead of structs can be more flexible.However, this approach is generally slower than using structs, so prefer strongly-typed structs when the structure is known.When precision matters, the json.Number type handles numeric JSON values without loss of precision.This approach prevents float64 conversion issues for large integers or precise decimal values.
Using Alternative Libraries Several high-performance JSON libraries exist for Go:These libraries often offer 30-80% performance improvements with minimal code changes.
Reducing Escaping Overhead The standard json package escapes HTML by default, which adds overhead.Disabling HTML escaping can significantly improve performance for payloads containing many special characters.
Practical Performance Example Here's a comprehensive example demonstrating multiple optimization techniques:This code demonstrates several optimization techniques: custom marshaling to avoid reflection, buffer pre-allocation to reduce memory churn, conditional field inclusion, and encoder reuse through sync.Pool.In my experience optimizing JSON handling for high-throughput systems, these techniques collectively reduced processing time by 70-90% and memory allocations by 50-80%. The most significant gains came from code generation and custom marshaling implementations.For one microservice processing 50,000 JSON documents per second, switching from the standard library to a combination of easyjson and custom marshaling reduced CPU utilization from 85% to 30% and cut latency by 65%.The ultimate optimization strategy depends on your specific use case. For most applications, start with field tag optimization and encoder pooling, then move to more complex techniques like custom marshaling or code generation if performance remains a bottleneck.With these techniques, Go's JSON processing can efficiently handle even the most demanding performance requirements, ensuring your applications remain responsive and resource-efficient at scale. is an AI-driven publishing company co-founded by author . By leveraging advanced AI technology, we keep our publishing costs incredibly low—some books are priced as low as —making quality knowledge accessible to everyone.Stay tuned for updates and exciting news. When shopping for books, search for to find more of our titles. Use the provided link to enjoy !Be sure to check out our creations:

item.title: Building a Production-Ready File Download System with GoFrame

item.content:encoded:
Hey devs! If you're building web applications with Go, you've probably encountered the need to handle file downloads. While it might seem straightforward at first, implementing production-ready file downloads comes with its own set of challenges. In this guide, I'll share practical insights on building robust file download functionality using GoFrame.TL;DR: We'll cover everything from basic file downloads to production-ready implementations with GoFrame, including handling large files, implementing resume support, and optimizing performance.Setting up basic file downloadsHandling large files without memory issuesImplementing resume/partial downloadsOptimizing for productionReal-world examples and gotchas
Why GoFrame for File Downloads? 🤔 Before we dive in, you might be wondering why choose GoFrame for handling file downloads. Here's the deal: while you could implement file downloads with standard Go libraries, GoFrame provides some nice abstractions that make our lives easier:Stream-based processing (goodbye memory issues!)Built-in middleware supportProgress tracking capabilitiesFirst things first, let's set up our environment. Make sure you have Go installed (1.16+), then grab GoFrame:go get github.com/gogf/gf/v2@latest Let's start with a simple file download handler. This is your entry point to understanding how GoFrame handles downloads:Pretty straightforward, right? But wait - there's more to consider when building for production! 🛠️Before we get too excited about serving files, let's talk security. Here's a robust validation function to prevent common security issues:
Handling Large Files Like a Pro 🏋️‍♂️ Now, here's where things get interesting. When dealing with large files, you don't want to load everything into memory. Here's a streaming implementation that won't make your server cry:
Resume Support: Because Downloads Sometimes Fail 😅 Let's be real - downloads can fail, especially for large files. Here's how to implement resume support:
Real-World Scenarios and Solutions 🎯 Let's look at some common scenarios you might encounter and how to handle them:
1. Handling Different File Types 📁 Different file types often need different handling. Here's a practical example:
2. Browser Compatibility Handling 🌐 Different browsers handle downloads differently. Here's how to handle it:
3. Download Progress Monitoring 📊 Here's how to implement progress monitoring with WebSocket updates:
Production Tips and Best Practices 💡 After implementing file downloads in numerous production environments, here are some battle-tested tips:Here's a practical monitoring implementation:Implement Circuit Breakers 🔌Protect your system with circuit breakers:Implement Graceful Shutdown 🛑Handle shutdowns properly:
Advanced Features and Examples 🚀
1. Zip Download on the Fly 📦 Need to create ZIP archives dynamically? Here's how:
2. Cloud Storage Integration 🌥️ Here's an example integrating with S3-compatible storage:
3. Download Queue Implementation 📋 For managing large numbers of downloads:
Comprehensive Error Handling Patterns 🎯 Let's look at how to handle various error scenarios robustly:
Specific Use Case Examples 🔍
1. Video Streaming with HLS Support 🎥
2. Large Excel File Generation 📊
3. PDF Generation and Download 📄
Common Gotchas to Avoid 🚨 Always use for cleanupDon't read entire files into memoryDon't forget to close files
Real-World Example: A Complete Download Service 🌟 Here's a production-ready download service combining all the concepts we've discussed:
Testing Your Implementation 🧪 Don't forget to test! Here's a quick test case to get you started:Building production-ready file downloads with GoFrame isn't just about calling . It's about:Handling security properlyManaging resources efficientlySupporting resume capabilitiesMonitoring and maintaining performanceAdd progress tracking for downloadsImplement cloud storage integrationAdd support for file preprocessingExplore streaming compressionLet me know in the comments if you have any questions or want to share your own experiences with file downloads in Go! 💬Like this article? Follow me for more Go development tips and tutorials! 🚀

item.title: Learn gRPC COMPLETELY by building a mini Exam Service in GoLang
item.author: Yashaswi Kumar Mishra
item.content:encoded:
Modern systems like Slack, Netfix, or even Kubernetes handle very efficiently. Their large and bulky backends are divided into . Have you ever wondered how these services communicate among themselves? Chances are that gRPC is behind the magic.In this blog, let us try to understand how can we implement a minimal Exam Service using gRPC in GoLang covering all types of RPCs: , , , and .If you already know what you are doing and just want the code, here is the repo.Before that let us actually try to undestand some basic concepts and simplify the jargon.Get ready for the complete gRPC guide.RPC or may sound like a fancy term it's just . Yes! But on other machine.Imagine you're the client and you want to ask a server: "Hey, what's the score for student ID 42?" RPC lets you do that as if you were calling a normal function, even though that function lives on a totally different system.Classic RPC systems came before gRPC, but they had a lot of baggage:Inconsistent data formats: No standard serialization; some used XML, some custom formats — all bulky and slow.: Supporting real-time or long-lived communication was hard or impossible.: Often tied to specific ecosystems (like Java RMI or CORBA).: Little to no auto-codegen — developers had to write boilerplate by hand.: No built-in encryption; you had to manage TLS yourself.: No multiplexing, no connection reuse — performance didn’t hold up under modern loads.In contrast, gRPC fixes these issues out of the box — faster, safer, and way easier to use.gRPC is an open-source RPC framework developed by Google. It's designed to make inter-service communication:Type-safe (via )Streaming-friendly (supports )
Okay. What the hell is HTTP/2? gRPC uses HTTP/2 under the hood. Compared to HTTP/1.1:It supports — multiple requests on one connection.Enables — both client and server can talk at the same time.Built-in — faster data transfer.This is how gRPC achieves low-latency real-time communication.gRPC supports four types of communication between the client and server. Let’s walk through each of them.
1. Unary RPC (Simple Request-Response) Client sends one request → Server sends one response.Think of it like a regular function call."Get me the marks of student with ID ."This is the most common type — perfect for operations.Client sends one request → Server sends a stream of responses.The server keeps pushing multiple responses over time."Give me the list of all exam results for semester 6."Useful when the server has a of data to send — you get results as they’re ready.Client sends a stream of requests → Server sends one final response.The client pushes a batch of data, and the server replies after processing it all."Here’s the attendance report of all students in bulk — store it."Great for sending logs, metrics, or bulk uploads.
4. Bidirectional Streaming RPC (Bidi) Client and server stream data to each other concurrently.Like a real-time chat where both can talk and listen at the same time."Start a live quiz session — the client sends answers, the server sends questions and scores in real time."This is where gRPC shines. Real-time multiplayer games, collaborative tools, live dashboards — it’s all possible.
The Last Bit of Jargon - Protocol Buffers Protocol Buffers or is a language-neutral, platform-neutral mechanism for serializing structured data — developed by Google.JSON is human-readable but .Protobuf is and faster to serialize/deserialize.JSON needs to parse field names like "student_id" every time. Protobuf uses tags (like 1, 2) behind the scenes for .You define your schema using a .proto file.The Protobuf compiler (protoc) generates code for your language (e.g., Go, Python, Java).That code provides you with methods to encode and decode messages efficiently.First of all let us bootstrap the project in go.grpc_exam grpc_exam go mod init github.com/pixperk/grpc_exam We will install the necessary dependencies (Go plugins for protoc)go google.golang.org/protobuf/cmd/protoc-gen-go@latest go google.golang.org/grpc/cmd/protoc-gen-go-grpc@latest If you do not have the protoc compiler installed, see the installion guide here.Let's ready our folder structure├── client/ │ ├── clients/ │ │ ├── unary.go │ │ ├── server_stream.go │ │ ├── client_stream.go │ │ ├── bi_stream.go │ └── main.go ├── proto/ │ ├── exam.proto │ └── generated/exampb/ │ ├── exam.pb.go │ └── exam_grpc.pb.go ├── server/ │ ├── main.go │ └── servers/ │ ├── unary.go │ ├── server_stream.go │ ├── client_stream.go │ ├── bi_stream.go │ └── exam_service_server.go ├── utils/ │ └── logger.go ├── go.mod ├── go.sum └── Makefile Let's write our makefile so that we do not have to write long commands everytime and just run protoc proto proto proto proto/.proto
go run server/main.go
go run client/main.go unary
go run client/main.go server
go run client/main.go client
go run client/main.go bidi
The proto command is for generating go code from the proto file.In the file, let us write our Exam Service.Now we run to generate our go code. Our code goes inside the proto/generated folder.In the server/servers/exam_service_server.go define thisLet's design the server and the client for this. First let's code the main.go inside the server and the client. The main.go's inside the server and client will drive them respectively.Now let's write the unary server and unary clientUnary Server (server/servers/unary.go) :Pretty easy right? Similarly Unary Client(client/clients/unary.go) :This is just basic function calls and simple go programming.You can see the results by running and in two separate terminals.Server Streaming and Client streaming are easy given you can handle streams in Go. Let's jump to bidirectional streaming and skim through streaming as well.
Building the Bidirectional RPC In the exam.proto file addIn the :Receives multiple exam queries from the client.Immediately responds to each with a result (if found).Continues until the client stops sending (EOF).Let's understand a bit about channels before proceeding to bi-directional client.Go channels are like pipes used to communicate between goroutines (lightweight threads). They help synchronize data exchange safely without using mutexes.Channels block until something is sent or received, making them perfect for goroutine coordination.
Now back to bi-dir client Let's discuss in bits about what goes on here (the function body) :LiveExamQuery opens a bidirectional stream with the server.done channel is created to signal when the receiver goroutine is finished. This goroutine listens for server responses and prints them.It runs in the background while the main thread handles user input. Reads user input (student_id exam_id).Sends each request to the server via the stream.If exit is typed, the client closes the send stream. Waits for the receiver goroutine to finish using the done channel.Ensures the program exits only after all communication is done.Now we can test the bidirectional RPC using the make commands like above.If you want to refer to any code or see the server or client streaming, this is the repo. Building a gRPC-based system with both server-side and client-side streaming is a great way to explore high-performance communication between services. Throughout this project, we learned how to:Define robust proto files and generate language-specific codeImplement server-side and client-side streaming using gRPCHandle bidirectional communication effectivelyStructure the project for maintainability and scalabilityWhether you're a beginner exploring RPC concepts or someone looking to implement efficient microservices communication, I hope this project offers a helpful starting point. Thanks for reading! Let me know if you have any questions down in the comments.

item.title: Introduction to Golang: A Comprehensive Guide for Beginners
item.author: Bharat Makwana
item.content:encoded:
Go, also known as Golang, is a powerful programming language developed at Google by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson. As a statically typed and compiled language, it combines performance with type safety. Go is celebrated for its simplicity, readability, and efficiency, featuring fast compilation and execution. Its built-in concurrency model, robust standard library, and garbage collection make it a favorite for modern software development.: Minimal syntax for easy comprehension.: Fast compilation and execution speeds.: Simplifies parallel task management.: Comprehensive tools for diverse applications.: Automatic memory management.Go’s clean and minimal syntax makes it approachable for beginners, while its high performance suits it for systems programming, web backends, and more. Its concurrency model, powered by goroutines and channels, simplifies complex tasks. Go’s growing ecosystem powers cloud-native technologies like Docker and Kubernetes, command-line tools, and microservices. Fast compile times and excellent tooling boost developer productivity.: Intuitive syntax reduces the learning curve.: Ideal for performance-critical applications.: Goroutines and channels streamline concurrent programming.: Widely used in modern tech stacks.: Rapid compilation and robust tools.
Setting Up Your Go Environment Getting started with Go is straightforward:: Visit https://go.dev/dl/ to download the installer for your operating system (Windows, macOS, or Linux).: Follow the OS-specific installation instructions.: Open a terminal and run to confirm Go is installed correctly.: Understand the structure (, , ). Note that Go Modules are now the standard for dependency management.
Your First Go Program: "Hello, World!" Let’s write a simple Go program to print "Hello, Bootcamp!" to the console:: Save this as and run in your terminal.:
: Defines an executable program.: Imports the formatted I/O library.: The program’s entry point.Go organizes code into packages. The package creates executable programs, while other packages (e.g., ) organize reusable code. Use to include external packages like or .Variables are declared using or the short declaration operator (inside functions only):Constants are immutable values defined with :Go supports basic data types:Floating-point: , String: (immutable UTF-8 text)Byte: (alias for )Go’s statements require braces but omit parentheses around conditions. An optional initialization statement can precede the condition:The loop is Go’s only looping construct, supporting multiple styles:Condition-only (while-like): Go’s statement is a cleaner alternative to multiple statements. It doesn’t fall through by default and supports multiple case values:Arrays are fixed-size sequences of elements of the same type. Their size is part of the type:Slices are dynamic, flexible views of arrays and are more commonly used. They support , , and :Maps store unordered key-value pairs, created with or literals:Structs group related fields to define custom data types:Functions in Go support parameters, return types, multiple return values, and variadic arguments:Pointers store memory addresses, using to get an address and to dereference:Pointers are useful for modifying function arguments or passing large structs efficiently.Methods are functions tied to a specific type (the receiver). Value receivers operate on copies, while pointer receivers modify the original:Interfaces define method signatures, enabling polymorphism without explicit implementation declarations:Types like or implicitly implement if they define . The empty interface can hold any value.Goroutines are lightweight, concurrent functions managed by the Go runtime:Channels enable communication and synchronization between goroutines:Go uses explicit error return values, with indicating success:The statement schedules cleanup tasks for when the surrounding function exits.
Standard Library Highlights Go’s standard library is rich and versatile, including:: Web servers and clients: JSON processing: File and system operations, : String manipulation: Time-related functions: Concurrency synchronization
Building and Running Go Programs : Compiles and runs without saving a binary.: Creates an executable binary in the current directory.: Compiles and installs the binary to or .To deepen your Go knowledge:: Manage dependencies with and .: Write tests using the package and .: Explore , , and .: Handle cancellations and deadlines.: Create a web service, CLI tool, or microservice.Go is a versatile, efficient, and beginner-friendly language with a thriving ecosystem. Its simplicity, performance, and concurrency features make it ideal for modern applications. Start experimenting with Go today, and join the growing community of developers building the future!

item.title: 5ST 5-R: The Fuse of Serenity
item.author: ersajay
item.content:encoded: In the enchanted workshops of Muggle electronics, where circuits hum like Floo Network whispers and overloads rage like Hungarian Horntails, there exists a guardian of unparalleled calm—the 5ST 5-R fuse. Like the Patronus of overcurrent protection, it whispers, “Breathe… then break,” shielding your PCB from chaos with Zen-like grace. Let us unravel the magic of this glass-tubed guru.Chapter 1: The Sorting Hat’s Chill Champion The 5ST 5-R is no ordinary Muggle fuse. Crafted by the Order of the Ohm Phoenix, this 5x20mm savior channels three ancient enchantments:Tempus Meditatio: Delays overloads by 0.1 seconds, granting currents a moment of Zen before Avada Kedavra-ing them. Eco-Guardian Charm: Halogen-free and RoHS-compliant—greener than a Herbology greenhouse. Ignis et Glacies: Survives -40°C blizzards and +125°C forge fires, tougher than a dragon’s scale.Why it outshines Muggle rivals:Fast-Acting Fuses: As subtle as a Confringo spell in a library. Polyfuses: Unreliable as a Time-Turner with a cracked hourglass.Chapter 2: The Triwizard Trials of OvercurrentThe Goblet of Inrush Currents (Power Supplies): When AC/DC converters roar like Hungarian Horntails, the 5ST 5-R casts Arresto Momentum, murmuring, “Namaste. Proceed… but gently.”The Chamber of Lithium Secrets (Battery Chargers): Halts overcharging lithium cells with the poise of Dumbledore disarming a wand. “Overload? Let’s discuss over tea.”The Forbidden Forest of Voltage Spikes (Monitors): Allows one tantrum from capacitors, then snaps like McGonagall shutting down Fred and George’s fireworks. The Marauder’s Map of Fuse WisdomZen Timing: 0.1-second delay—faster than a Snitch, calmer than a Hufflepuff. Miniature Mastery: Smaller than a Knut, yet stops 250V meltdowns. Eco-Sorcery: RoHS-compliant, because even fuses care about the Forbidden Forest.The Dark Arts (Fuse Foibles)One-Time Spell: Unlike a Protego charm, it won’t reset—keep spares, or face a Howler from your PCB. Overload Dementors: Push past 5A, and even this guru’s patience snaps.The Prophecy of Circuit Peace Sybill Trelawney’s Murmurings:“Beware the fast-acting fuse… a harbinger of chaos!” “Only the 5ST 5-R, the Serenity Fuse, shall balance the currents of fate.”Dumbledore’s Wisdom: “Happiness can be found even in the darkest of overloads, if one remembers to use a 5ST 5-R.”Epilogue: A Guardian’s Oath To ignore the 5ST 5-R is to court disaster—a realm of fried traces and smoky regrets. Choose wisely, for as Hermione once hissed: “It’s levi-OH-sa, not levio-SA—precision matters!”Advanced Protection Charms (Hogwarts Press, 2025) The Tales of Beedle the Fuse-Wright (Weasleys’ Workshop Editions) Hogwarts: A History of Circuit Serenity (Ministry of Tech)

item.title: Problems with Time-Based Code & How Fake Clocks Solve Them
item.author: Mohammad Zeya Ahmad
item.content:encoded: ⚠️ Problems with Time-Based Code in TestsWhen writing Go programs, we often rely on like:These are useful for implementing timeouts, periodic tasks, and scheduled operations. However, they introduce several :If a function waits before timing out, your test takes .This slows down and .2️⃣ Flaky Tests (Intermittent Failures)Real-world execution times due to CPU load, network latency, and system clock drift.Example: A timeout-based test might pass on a but fail on a .You can’t or .Makes it timeouts, retries, and delays.4️⃣ Hard to Test Race ConditionsGo routines using real time can , leading to tests.To solve these problems, we use a instead of the real package. Fake clocks allow us to:✅ instead of waiting in real-time.Ensure predictable timing behavior in tests., improving speed.Simulate timeouts, delays, and retries deterministically.Let's say we have a function that waits for an event but times out after if nothing happens.Running this test would If you had , your test suite would take to complete.go get github.com/benbjohnson/clock 📌 Rewriting the function using a Fake Clock:✅ No need to wait for 5 seconds.A ticker prints : → .📌 Rewriting the function using Fake Clock:✅ We instead of waiting.Slow tests (timeouts/tickers)Use to fast-forward timeFlaky tests (random failures) for consistent behaviorUncontrollable race conditions using Fake ClocksSimulate retries instantlyFake clocks make testing time-dependent code easy, fast, and reliable! 🚀Here are some great resources to learn more about testing time-dependent code in Go using fake clocks: – A deep dive into the concept of fake clocks with Go code examples. – Explains different approaches to testing time-based code.Effective Testing for Go Applications – Discusses mock implementations in Go, including fake clocks.📚 Official Documentation & LibrariesGo package documentation – Understanding the standard package is essential before using fake clocks.

item.title: Some signal boosting for Go

item.title: Why You Can’t Access Struct Fields Through Interfaces in Go (and How to Fix It)
item.author: Shams Pathan
item.content:encoded:
Ever written something like this in Go?...and thought, “Wait — why can’t I access when it’s clearly part of the struct?”In Go, interfaces are , not containers for struct fields. They only guarantee access to methods, not fields. So when you assign a struct to an interface, Go stores it as a hidden value — only accessible if you:
🧪 Real-World Example: API Payload Let’s say you’re writing an API handler that receives some payload:Yep — Go won’t let you access . Because doesn’t know exists.
✅ Solution: Type Assertion You can safely access the struct’s fields if you assert its type:Or if you’re not sure of the type, use a type switch:
⚠️ Or... Use Reflection (Carefully) You also access struct fields dynamically via , like:But reflection can be tricky and error-prone. Use it only when you use static typing — like dynamic JSON decoding or generic form handlers.Go’s strict typing can feel limiting at first, but it protects you from a whole class of bugs. Once you understand interfaces don’t “carry” struct fields — just methods — it all starts to click.Want to go deeper? I shared the full breakdown with code samples and reflection examples here:Have you ever hit this issue in a real project? How did you work around it? Let’s chat below 👇

item.title: ShopCTL: A Developer-First Toolkit for Shopify Automation
item.author: Konsole
item.content:encoded:
Shopify at your fingertips with shell automationLearning Shopify has been on my bucket list for a few years now. Plenty of people in my circle — friends, colleagues, and fellow devs — are all somehow involved with Shopify in one way or the other. Earlier this year, I finally had some breathing room between projects, so I figured it was the perfect time to give Shopify a proper look.I started exploring the platform by setting up a dev store, poking around the admin, and skimming through the API manual. While this was a quick and easy start, it didn’t give me a deeper understanding of the platform. Plus, clicking my way through the UI felt repetitive and tedious.That got me thinking: is there a more efficient, developer-centric way to manage a store? Something that I could run in a terminal, plug into a CI/CD pipeline, or script my way out of those mundane tasks.[WiP] Command line Utility for Shopify Data ManagementShopCTL is a slightly opinionated, in-progress command-line utility for managing your Shopify store data. It comes with a handful of easy-to-compose commands giving you a quick way to interact with your store's data straight from the terminal.Add http://127.0.0.1/shopctl/auth/callback to the list of Allowed redirection URL(s)Install the runnable binary to your ..@ will be available as a packaged downloadable binary for different platforms later.Before you begin using ShopCTL, you need to configure your store(s). ShopCTL supports managing multiple Shopify stores by using contexts — each…ShopCTL is an in-progress command-line utility for managing your Shopify store data. It comes with a handful of easy-to-compose commands, giving you a quick way to interact with your store’s data straight from the terminal.Simply put, it’s like a Swiss Army knife for your Shopify store — giving you quick, scriptable commands to query and modify products, customers, and more (upcoming).ShopCTL currently comes with product and customer-related commands. The tool is POSIX-compliant, giving you a familiarity with standard Unix command-line operations. The CLI flags are designed such that you can combine available flags in any order to create a unique query. For instance, the command below will give you all gift-cards on status DRAFT that were created after January 2025 and have tags on-sale and premium.shopctl product list on-sale,premium The list commands also accept queries in a Shopify Search query syntax as the first argument. That means you can build a complex search query as needed for your use case. shopctl product list premium In addition to advanced searching, you can create, update, delete, export, and import products — including their options, variants, and media — and most of these features also apply to customers. A handy peek command is available to give you a quick glance at product details right from your terminal. Support for other resources might be added in the future as needed.
Automation and Scripting Use-cases Let’s explore some possible real-life use cases.True power in scripting isn’t just about automating tasks — it’s the belief that even the simplest code can create wonders. — Not meSeasonal sales are great for business, but updating prices for a whole collection can be tedious. A CLI tool could make it easy to script bulk price adjustments. For example, suppose you want to apply a 30% discount to all products tagged with “summer-sale” for a summer sale:product_id shopctl product list summer-sale shopctl product variant list ,price | variant_id price | bc shopctl product variant edit
Automated Inventory Discounts for Clearance Ever have excess inventory you want to clear out? With ShopCTL, you can easily look for and flag or discount overstocked items. Let’s say any product with more than 100 units in stock should get a 20% price drop and a “clearance” tag. We can achieve this by filtering on inventory and then updating those products:product_id shopctl product list shopctl product variant list ,price | variant_id price | bc shopctl product variant edit shopctl product update
Data Cleanup & Dynamic Tagging Data cleanup is another area where scripting shines. As your product catalog grows, keeping track of what’s selling and what’s just sitting in inventory becomes harder. Why not tag these products based on specific conditions, like high inventory and older creation dates, and act upon it without manually digging through product listings? shopctl product list | xargs shopctl product update slow-moving Another use-case could be to archive products with missing SKUs. Suppose you want to find any products that have missing SKUs (perhaps products that were created without proper variant SKUs) and archive them so they don’t clutter your active catalog. You can search for products with empty SKU fields and update their status in bulk with some simple bash scripts:product_id shopctl product list shopctl product update archived How about something as simple as quickly finding and deleting stale DRAFT products to keep your catalog clean? shopctl product list — created “< +%Y-%m-%d” | xargs shopctl product delete
Attaching Media to Products via CSV Attaching images or other media to products in bulk can be cumbersome through the web UI. Let’s say a client provides you a CSV file with relevant columns like a product ID/handle and a URL to an image that needs to be attached to that product. You can perform media attachments in bulk with a simple bash script. product_id,image_url,alt,media_type 12345,https://example.com/image1.jpg,Front view,image 12345,https://example.com/video1.mp4,Demo video,video 56789,https://example.com/image1.jpg,Front view,image
+2 images.csv | , product_id image_url alt media_type shopctl product media attach
Exporting and Importing Customer Data Last but not least, consider the task of exporting customer data for a marketing campaign or migrating customers to a new store. ShopCTL has you covered with built-in export/import for customers as well. For example, to export all customers from one store and import them into another:shopctl store1 /backups customers_this_quarter shopctl import store2 customer /backups/customers_this_quarter.tar.gz The possibilities with scripting are endless — you can shape it to fit your own workflows and solve unique use cases. The examples above are just the start. As the tool matures, it will open the door to some advanced automation and workflows.Check out the project page to view the full set of features and learn more about the project.Your suggestions and feedback are highly appreciated. Feel free to start a discussion or create an issue to share your experience with the tool or to discuss a feature/issue. If you think this project is useful, consider contributing by starring the repo, sharing it with your friends, or submitting a PR.

item.title: A basic Golang project Makefile
item.author: DevByJ
item.content:encoded:
In some of my side projects, I develop with Golang. The CLI tool Make is mainly used there to execute build and test commands, but it is also used for other tasks.After some time, the following has become established with me, which I would like to share. ./coverage ./build
egrep go vet ./...
go ./...
COVERAGE_DIRgo count COVERAGE_DIR/cover.out ./... go tool cover COVERAGE_DIR/cover.out COVERAGE_DIR/cover.html open COVERAGE_DIR/cover.html
go run .
go build BUILD_DIR/app
COVERAGE_DIRBUILD_DIRMy goal was to store and execute all the commands I need for development in Make. The “target with prerequisites mechanism” is not used here.Basically, behind most targets are commands with Go tools. These are very powerful, because in addition to code execution and build, there are Go tools for analysis as well as testing and testing with coverage view in the browser included. In addition, there is the management of to-do notes and the deletion of the coverage and build directories.

item.title: Mastering Go's Empty Interface: Powerful Uses and Hidden Traps
item.author: Shrijith Venkatramana
item.content:encoded:
Hi there! I'm Shrijith Venkatrama, founder of Hexmos. Right now, I’m building LiveAPI, a tool that makes generating API docs from your code ridiculously easy.Go’s empty interface, , is one of those tools that feels like a superpower at first glance. It can hold —numbers, strings, structs, you name it. But with great power comes... well, a bunch of headaches if you’re not careful. In this post, we’ll dive into what makes tick, explore its legitimate use cases, and flag the traps that can make your code messy or slow. Expect plenty of examples, some tables to clarify things, and tips to keep your Go code clean and robust. Let’s get started.
What Is ? Go’s Catch-All Type The empty interface, written as , is a special type in Go that has . Since any type in Go implements at least zero methods, every value in Go satisfies . This makes it a universal container, letting you store or pass around values of any type without knowing what they are upfront.Sounds amazing, right? You can throw anything into an and deal with it later. But this flexibility comes at a cost: , and that can lead to runtime errors or hard-to-read code. Before we get to the pitfalls, let’s look at where shines.
Where Saves the Day: Real-World Use Cases The empty interface is handy when you need flexibility that Go’s strict type system doesn’t easily allow. Here are three common scenarios where it’s a lifesaver, complete with examples.
Storing Mixed Types in Collections Sometimes, you need a slice or map to hold different types—like integers, strings, or custom structs. A or makes this possible.: Let’s create a slice with mixed types and print each value.Value: 42, Type: int Value: golang, Type: string Value: true, Type: bool Value: {Alice}, Type: struct { Name string } This is great for prototyping or when you’re handling data with unpredictable types. But you’ll often need type assertions (like ) to work with the values, which we’ll cover later.
Writing Functions That Handle Any Type Ever wonder how can print integers, strings, and structs without breaking a sweat? It uses under the hood. You can write similar functions that accept any type.: A simple function to log any value.Logged: 100 Logged: Hello, Go! Logged: [1 2 3] This approach is perfect for utility functions that don’t care about the input’s specific type, like logging or formatting tools.
Parsing Dynamic Data Like JSON When dealing with JSON or YAML where the structure isn’t known in advance, is your go-to. Libraries like use it to unmarshal data into or .: Unmarshaling JSON with an unknown schema.Name: Bob, Age: 30, Hobbies: [coding gaming] Here, lets us handle JSON without defining a struct upfront. But accessing fields requires type assertions, which can get messy if the data structure is complex.
The Dark Side of : Pitfalls to Avoid As useful as is, it’s easy to overuse or misuse. Here are the big traps to watch out for, with examples to show what goes wrong.
Throwing Away Type Safety Go’s type system is there to catch errors at compile time. Using bypasses that, pushing errors to runtime. If you rely on type assertions, one wrong assumption can cause a panic.: A risky type assertion.To avoid this, you can use a type switch or safe assertions:: Type Assertion vs. Type SwitchAvoids panics with checkHandles multiple types cleanlyOverkill for single-type checksUsing involves runtime type information, which can slow things down. Every time you store a value in an , Go wraps it in an interface structure. Type assertions and switches add more overhead.: Compare a typed slice vs. an slice.The version is slower due to type assertions. For small datasets, it’s negligible, but it adds up in loops or large collections.
Making Code Hard to Follow When you use everywhere, it’s like saying, “This function accepts .” Readers (including future you) have to dig through the code to figure out what types are expected.: A vague function.Contrast that with a clear interface:The second version tells you exactly what’s needed: something with a method.
Using Without Regrets: Best Practices To keep from turning your codebase into a mess, follow these guidelines:. Only reach for when you genuinely need to handle unknown types, like parsing dynamic JSON.. If a function takes , clarify what types are valid in comments or docs.. Always use safe assertions or type switches to avoid panics.. Can a specific interface or generics solve the problem better?: Refining a vague function with a custom interface.This ensures only types with a method are passed, keeping things clear and safe.
Beyond : Smarter Alternatives Since Go 1.18, have given us a type-safe way to write flexible code without . You can also define precise interfaces for specific needs. Let’s see how.
Generics for Flexible Collections Instead of , use a generic slice to maintain type safety.: A generic print function.The constraint is similar to , but it’s checked at compile time, so you avoid runtime surprises.
Custom Interfaces for Clarity If you know what behavior you need, define an interface instead of using .: A logging interface.This is clearer than and ensures only types with a method are used.: vs. AlternativesDynamic data, prototypingFlexible collections, algosThe empty interface is a powerful tool, but it’s not a one-size-fits-all solution. Use when you need ultimate flexibility, like handling JSON with unknown schemas or writing utility functions like . But don’t let it become a crutch—favor type safety and clarity with custom interfaces or generics whenever possible.By keeping in check, you’ll write Go code that’s easier to maintain, faster to run, and less likely to crash at 3 a.m. Experiment with the examples above, and next time you’re tempted to slap on a function, ask yourself:
